front.title: Guidelines for Evaluating AI-Driven Network Operations Work in the IETF

abstract.t: A growing number of individual Internet-Drafts address the use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) in network operations and management. These efforts are currently spread across multiple working groups and mailing lists without a coordinated standards-track path. This document surveys the current landscape, identifies what the IETF is well-positioned to standardize, and provides a framework to help WG chairs, Area Directors (AD), and the broader community evaluate individual submissions and identify categories of work that are appropriate for IETF standardization. The existing landscape of individual drafts is used as evidence to ground these categories in current community activity; the disposition of any individual submission remains a matter for community consensus.

note.name: Discussion Venues
note.t: Discussion of this document should take place on the OPSAWG mailing list (opsawg@ietf.org) or the AINETOPS mailing list (ainetops@ietf.org).

section.title: Introduction
section.t: AI and ML techniques are increasingly being applied to network operations: anomaly detection, closed-loop remediation, intent translation, and autonomous device management. The IETF community has responded with a growing number of individual drafts exploring governance frameworks , network management agent architectures , use cases , and research challenges .

However, these efforts remain disconnected. There is no chartered working group, no agreed set of deliverables, and no clear standards-track path for the work. Without coordination, the community risks producing overlapping informational documents rather than interoperable standards.

This document provides a framework to help WG chairs, area directors, and the broader community evaluate individual submissions and determine whether a given effort is well-positioned for IETF standardization. It identifies where the IETF can make a unique and concrete contribution — specifically through YANG data models, protocol extensions, and operational guidance — and describes categories of work that are appropriate for IETF standardization. It also identifies areas that are better left to research, industry forums, or other bodies. The existing landscape of individual drafts described in is used as evidence to identify these categories, not to prescribe the disposition of any individual document, which remains a matter for community consensus.

section.title: Conventions and Definitions
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

section.title: Current Landscape
section.t: The following efforts are relevant to AI-driven network operations in the IETF and IRTF:

ul.li: proposes a governance framework of thirteen principles for AI-mediated autonomous network device management, covering human authority, safety, auditability, reversibility, and bounded autonomy.

defines a Network Management Agent (NMA) concept, an AI- driven entity that bridges service intent and network operations using an Intent-Awareness-Analysis-Decision- Execution (IAADE) control loop. The draft identifies three interfaces — Agent-to-User (A2U), Agent-to-Controller (A2C), and Agent-to-Network (A2N) — but explicitly defers the definition of the A2C interface to future documents.
documents use cases including reactive troubleshooting, proactive assurance, closed-loop optimization, misconfiguration detection, and virtual operator assistance.
identifies research challenges for coupling AI with network management, including distribution of AI tasks and privacy concerns.
The AINETOPS non-WG mailing list (ainetops@ietf.org) was established to discuss integration of AI and ML in network operations using IETF technologies.

argues that NETCONF has five architectural gaps for AI-driven operations — AI semantic translation, long-lived cross-domain orchestration, multi-agent consensus, rapid DevOps iteration, and large artifact delivery — and proposes the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol as complementary layers alongside NETCONF, not replacements for it.

performs a gap analysis of NETCONF and RESTCONF for LLM-driven intent- based networking, identifying the absence of a YANG-to-JSON- Schema automation pipeline as a blocking gap: LLMs cannot reason over YANG tree notation but can consume JSON Schema tool descriptors.

specifies concrete MCP extensions for network equipment management, defining tools that map directly onto YANG datastore operations (get, edit, commit, rollback) and capability advertisement that lets MCP servers declare their supported YANG modules.

proposes a framework for LLM-assisted network management with a mandatory human- in-the-loop audit step before any AI-generated configuration is deployed, and defines YANG modules for both the LLM response record and the human approval workflow.

section.t: Collectively, these efforts have established the problem space and use cases. The next step is to translate this into standards-track work with concrete, interoperable deliverables.

section.title: Where IETF Can Contribute
section.t: The IETF's established strengths lie in defining YANG data models, protocol behavior, and operational guidelines. Applied to AI-driven network operations, this means:

ul.li: Standardizing the data models that describe how AI agents are configured, governed, and monitored — so that operators can manage AI agents through the same NETCONF/RESTCONF interfaces they already use for network devices.
Extending existing management protocols to carry attribution metadata for AI-initiated changes, enabling interoperable audit trails and rollback.
Producing operational security guidance as a BCP, so that operators deploying AI agents can follow a common baseline regardless of vendor implementation.

section.t: The IETF is not well-positioned to specify AI algorithms, model architectures, or training methodologies. Those topics belong in the IRTF or in industry forums.

section.title: Evaluation Framework and Work Categories
section.t: The following sections describe categories of work that are well-suited to IETF standardization, along with criteria that chairs and the community can use to evaluate whether a specific submission falls within that category. The existing submissions described in are referenced as examples to ground these categories in current community activity. Whether any given submission should be adopted, where it should be placed, and on what track it should be published are matters for community consensus, not this document.

section.title: Criteria for Evaluating Proposed Work Items
section.t: When evaluating whether to adopt a submission as a work item — whether in an existing WG such as OPSAWG or NMOP, or as part of a proposal for a new WG — chairs and area directors should consider the following criteria:

ul.li: Proposed work items should commit to producing specific data models, protocol extensions, or BCP guidance. General architectural descriptions and high-level surveys are more appropriate for IRTF or IAB publications and are not strong candidates for adoption as WG work items.

Gap analyses are appropriate work items only when directly coupled to a committed standards-track or BCP deliverable in the same body of work. Standalone gap analyses that are not coupled to a normative work item do not advance the standards track and are not strong candidates for adoption.

A use cases and requirements document is a valuable early work item to scope and prioritize normative deliverables. Whether such a document is published as an RFC or serves as an internal reference is a community decision.
Proposals for new work should explicitly delineate what is out of scope, in particular AI and ML algorithm specification, which belongs in the IRTF.

section.title: YANG Data Models
section.t: YANG data models represent a primary category of work appropriate for IETF standardization. The following subsections describe four distinct areas of AI agent management where YANG data models would produce concrete, interoperable deliverables. Current submissions in provide evidence that the problem space and required data structures are sufficiently well understood to support standards-track YANG work in each of these areas.

section.title: Intent Submission YANG Model
section.t: specifies that operators and peer agents submit intents to the NMA via the Agent-to-User (A2U) interface using structured formats such as YANG or JSON. The intent carries a high-level operational goal (e.g., "restore SLA for service X" or "optimize traffic across domain Y") along with associated constraints such as latency bounds, cost limits, and affected resource scope.

Standardizing this intent submission format as a YANG data model is well within IETF scope, covering: the intent itself, the constraints attached to it, the submitting entity (operator or peer agent), the target scope, and the status of intent fulfillment. Such a model would provide the northbound interface through which human operators and orchestration systems interact with AI agents in a vendor-neutral way, and would serve as the input to the IAADE control loop described in .

section.title: AI Agent Audit Log YANG Model
section.t: specifies in detail what an audit trail for an AI agent must contain: action timestamps, the anomaly that triggered the action, AI prompts and responses, pre- and post-action state snapshots, the outcome of the action, and whether the action was taken autonomously or required human approval.

This is a well-defined schema, and standardizing it as a YANG data model is well within IETF scope: it would ensure that AI agent audit logs are interoperable across vendors and consumable by existing operational tooling. A minimum retention period of 365 days SHOULD be captured in the model as a configurable default.

The model SHOULD also capture the human-in- the-loop workflow: whether a proposed action required human approval before execution, the identity of the approver, the timestamp of approval or rejection, and any operator annotation attached to the decision. This extends the audit record beyond what the AI agent did autonomously to include the governance interactions that constrained it, enabling post-hoc analysis of where human oversight was exercised and where it was not.

section.title: AI Agent Configuration and Governance YANG Model
section.t: The governance parameters described in — the Action Registry, operator-managed Allow List and Block List, rate limit parameters (actions per hour, per-target limits, cooldown periods), protected resource targets, and degradation level thresholds — are all operator-configured state.

Standardizing these as a YANG data model is well within IETF scope: it would allow operators to configure and audit AI agent governance through the same NETCONF/RESTCONF interfaces they already use, and would enable consistent policy enforcement across heterogeneous deployments.

section.title: AI Agent Operational State YANG Model
section.t: The runtime state of an AI agent — its current degradation level, active rate-limit counters, retry counts per target, escalation state, and current execution mode (autonomous versus monitoring-only) — constitutes operational state that operators need to observe.

This model is further informed by the IAADE control loop defined in . The operational state SHOULD include the current phase of the IAADE loop (Intent, Awareness, Analysis, Decision, or Execution), the active intent being processed, and for each phase: the inputs consumed, the outputs produced, and any exceptions encountered. This gives operators visibility into not only the health of the AI agent but also what it is currently doing and why.

Standardizing this as a YANG operational state model is well within IETF scope: it would allow operators to monitor AI agent health and activity through existing telemetry and streaming telemetry infrastructure, using the same tools they use to observe network device state.

section.title: NETCONF/RESTCONF Extensions for AI Agents
section.t: Protocol extensions to NETCONF and RESTCONF represent a second category of work that is well within IETF scope. Current submissions have identified three gaps in these protocols for AI-driven network management.

First, when an AI agent modifies device configuration via NETCONF or RESTCONF, there is currently no standard way to record that the change was AI-initiated in the datastore commit journal or in NETCONF notification streams. Defining a metadata attribute for NETCONF <edit-config> operations and RESTCONF PATCH requests that identifies the change as AI-initiated, carries the agent identity, and references the audit log entry that triggered the action would address this gap. A corresponding extension to NETCONF notifications to carry this attribution would allow downstream consumers such as network management systems and SIEM tools to correlate configuration events with AI agent activity.

Second, identifies that the controller northbound interface needs to support intent-based RPCs — operations that accept a high-level operational goal (e.g., "optimize-performance for service X") rather than specific configuration parameters, and return status on goal fulfillment. This is qualitatively different from the request-response model of existing NETCONF RPCs and would require new RPC definitions that can carry structured intents as inputs and fulfillment status as outputs.

Third, an NMA may run multiple concurrent management sessions for independent intents, each requiring strict context isolation to prevent state interference between parallel tasks. NETCONF's existing session model does not provide this isolation natively. A lightweight context identifier mechanism for NETCONF sessions that allows a single transport connection to multiplex independent agent task contexts would address this gap.

Work of this type — protocol extensions anchored in existing IETF management protocols — is well within IETF scope. The NETCONF WG has directly relevant expertise for such work, and coordination with WGs focused on YANG data models and network management would be appropriate.

section.title: MCP Extensions for Network Management
section.t:
The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an emerging interface layer designed to allow LLM-based agents to discover and invoke management functions through machine-readable tool descriptors. Three OPSAWG drafts
have identified MCP as a complementary interface that addresses gaps in NETCONF and RESTCONF for AI-driven scenarios, specifically: AI semantic translation, LLM- readable capability discovery, and DevOps-paced tool registration. MCP does not replace NETCONF or RESTCONF; it provides a semantic interface layer above them.

Two MCP-related items represent appropriate IETF work in this category. First, a mapping of YANG datastores and NETCONF/RESTCONF operations to MCP tool schemas, such that an MCP server backed by a NETCONF/RESTCONF agent exposes operations like get, edit-config, commit, and rollback as JSON-Schema-described tools with consistent parameter names and error codes. provides a concrete starting point that illustrates what such work would look like.

Second, a YANG module for MCP capability advertisement that allows a network device or controller to declare, through its existing NETCONF/RESTCONF management interface, which YANG modules it supports, which operations are available as MCP tools, and what rollback and confirmation capabilities are present. This would allow AI agents to query device capabilities using the same infrastructure they use for configuration, without requiring a separate capability discovery protocol.

Work of this type — defining standard interfaces between AI-facing protocol layers and existing IETF management protocols — is well within IETF scope. WGs focused on network management and YANG data models would have relevant expertise, and coordination with the NETCONF WG would be appropriate for any extensions to NETCONF notification semantics that MCP tool responses may require.

section.title: Agent-to-Agent and Agent-to-Controller Interfaces
section.t: identifies two interfaces that require standardization in the independent NMA deployment mode: the Agent-to-Controller (A2C) interface between the NMA and an existing SDN controller, and — implicitly — peer Agent-to-Agent (A2A) interfaces for multi-agent coordination in heterogeneous deployments.

Interface definitions of this type are well within IETF scope when expressed using existing IETF management protocols (NETCONF or RESTCONF) and YANG data models, avoiding the introduction of a new protocol. For the A2C case, such work would define how the NMA invokes controller capabilities (topology queries, telemetry subscriptions, configuration enforcement) as abstract tools, and how the controller returns results and exceptions to the NMA. WGs focused on network management protocol extensions and operations have directly relevant expertise for this category of work.

Beyond the single NMA-to-controller case, real deployments involve multiple AI agents operating concurrently across domains, each with different capabilities and authorities.
identifies multi-agent consensus and long-lived cross-domain orchestration as gaps not addressed by NETCONF. Defining how AI agents advertise their capabilities, negotiate task delegation, and exchange structured results in a way that is interoperable across vendors and anchored in existing IETF management protocols is appropriate IETF work. Such work would typically include: a YANG module for agent capability advertisement, a mechanism for task delegation and status reporting between peer agents using NETCONF/RESTCONF, and the trust and authorization model governing agent-to-agent interactions.
Both the A2C and A2A categories benefit from coordination between WGs focused on network management protocol extensions and those focused on YANG data model work.

section.title: Operational Security BCP
section.t: The security considerations specific to AI-mediated network management are qualitatively different from those of traditional management plane access. They include:

ul.li: The AI service as an attack surface: prompt injection via device-generated telemetry strings included in AI prompts, and compromise of the AI service API endpoint.
Credential management: AI agents require management plane credentials that must not appear in plaintext configuration or AI prompts.
Audit trail integrity: write-ahead logging with integrity protection and forwarding to external aggregators to prevent tampering.
Exfiltration risk: AI agents that send telemetry to external AI services may inadvertently exfiltrate topology, credential, or customer data.

section.t: A BCP document addressing these considerations is well within IETF scope. It would give operators deploying AI agents a normative security baseline independent of vendor implementation. WGs focused on operational guidance for network management would have directly relevant expertise for this category of work.

section.title: Governance Principles as Informational Work
section.t: Governance principle documents represent a distinct category of work appropriate for IETF publication. When a submission defines a well-structured, broadly applicable set of principles for AI-mediated network management — covering areas such as human authority, safety, auditability, reversibility, and bounded autonomy — it can provide a stable reference foundation for the normative work described in the other categories in this section. Submissions in this category are strong candidates for Informational publication if the community reaches consensus on their content.

The normative content that implements governance principles — the specific configurations, data models, and protocol behaviors — is better expressed in the YANG data models described in than in the principles document itself. is an example of current community work in this category.

section.title: Out of Scope
section.t: The following are explicitly out of scope for the proposed IETF work:

dl.dd: High-level architecture descriptions of how AI fits into network management are better suited to IRTF or IAB documents. IETF work should start from a defined architecture and produce data models and protocol extensions for it, not define the architecture itself.

Specifying which algorithms to use for anomaly detection, classification, or remediation recommendation is outside IETF scope. The YANG data models and protocol extensions proposed here are intentionally AI-service-agnostic.
dl.dt: Gap analyses and surveys without committed deliverables
dl.dd: Gap analyses that are directly coupled to a committed standards-track or BCP work item in the same body of work are in scope. Gap analyses that stand alone — without an associated data model or protocol document — are not strong candidates for adoption as work items, as the use cases and requirements document is sufficient to scope the normative work.

Guidelines for aligning IETF YANG data models with TMF, 3GPP, or other external frameworks are outside IETF scope and should be addressed through liaison statements rather than adopted as WG work items.

section.title: Summary of Work Categories
section.t: The following table summarizes the categories of work identified in this document. Whether any specific submission falls within a given category, and what should happen to it, are matters for community evaluation using the criteria in .

tr.td: Use cases and requirements
RFC or internal reference (community decision)
Scope and prioritize normative work; should not stand alone as a work item endpoint
YANG data models for AI agent management (intent, audit, governance, operational state)
Standards Track
Defines interoperable, vendor-neutral data structures for AI agent management via NETCONF/RESTCONF
NETCONF/RESTCONF protocol extensions for AI agents
Standards Track
Extends existing protocols; avoids introduction of new protocols; addresses identified gaps
MCP-to-YANG interface layer
Standards Track
Defines standard mappings between AI-facing protocol layers and existing IETF management protocols
Agent-to-Controller and Agent-to-Agent interfaces
Standards Track
Anchored in existing IETF management protocols; defines capability advertisement, delegation, and trust
Operational security BCP for AI-mediated management
BCP
Normative baseline for operators; not vendor- or algorithm-specific
Governance principles
Informational
Broad community consensus on principles; normative specifics expressed in YANG models
Gap analyses
In scope only when coupled to a committed deliverable
Must be coupled to an associated standards-track or BCP work item in the same body of work

section.title: Operational Considerations
section.t: Operators deploying AI agents for network management should ensure that the management interfaces used by AI agents are subject to the same access control, logging, and change management policies as human operator access. The YANG data models proposed in are designed to integrate with existing NETCONF/RESTCONF management infrastructure rather than requiring separate management planes for AI agents.

section.title: Security Considerations
section.t: The security considerations for AI-mediated network management are discussed in . Key concerns include prompt injection via device-generated data, credential exposure to AI services, audit trail integrity, and telemetry exfiltration. These are addressed in detail in .

section.title: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document has no IANA actions.

section.title: Acknowledgments
section.t: The framework in this document was informed by discussions on the OPSAWG and AINETOPS mailing lists, and by the presentations at IETF 125. The governance framework in by Andy Smith and Nalin Pai provided the concrete foundation from which the YANG data model categories are derived.
